params.text:
Recognising the symptoms

Being aware of the symptoms of Legionnaires' disease helps you act quickly if someone you support becomes unwell. The illness often begins like a bad case of flu before it affects the lungs, so the early signs can be easy to miss.

  • Flu-like start: high temperature, chills, headache and aching muscles.
  • Chest signs: a cough, breathlessness and sometimes chest pain.
  • Other signs: confusion, tiredness, and sometimes sickness or diarrhoea.

When to raise the alarm

Legionnaires' disease usually starts with flu-like symptoms — a high temperature, chills, headache and muscle aches — and then develops into a cough, difficulty breathing and sometimes chest pain. Some people also become confused or develop sickness or diarrhoea. Symptoms generally appear a few days after exposure.

You are not expected to diagnose anyone. What matters is that if someone becomes unwell with these symptoms, you report it promptly to a senior colleague or nurse so they can arrange medical help. If more than one person on the same unit develops a chest infection, mention that too — a possible link is exactly the kind of thing the responsible person needs to know about.

params.question: A resident develops a high temperature, muscle aches, a cough and breathlessness. What should you do?

answers.text: Report it promptly to a senior colleague or nurse so that medical help can be arranged.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Prompt reporting gets the person the medical assessment they need.

answers.text: Decide yourself that it is Legionnaires' disease and tell the resident.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: You are not expected to diagnose — report the symptoms so professionals can assess.

answers.text: Wait several days to see whether the symptoms clear up on their own.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Waiting risks serious harm; these symptoms need reporting now.

answers.text: Assume it cannot be serious because chest infections are common.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: These symptoms should always be taken seriously and reported.
